1: wayyinnāgᵉpû yiśrāʾēl — ‘And Israel was struck/defeated’ (Niphal of nāgaph). The Niphal passive frames Israel as the recipient of military calamity, not the agent — YHWH stands behind the defeat. wayyippᵉlû ḥᵃlālîm — ‘falling as slain/pierced ones’ (ḥālāl, the slain in battle, often with connotations of dishonor). har haggîlbōaʿ — Mount Gilboa is the site; the Chronicler’s version of the battle (parallel to 1 Sam 31) is briefer, omitting the prior night at Endor (1 Sam 28), because the Chronicler’s purpose is the theological verdict in vv13-14, not the narrative details.
2: wayyadbᵉqû pᵉlištîm — ‘the Philistines pressed close after/clung to Saul’ (Qal imperfect of dāḇaq, usually used for covenant loyalty, as in Ruth 1:14 or Deuteronomy 4:4). The ironic use of dāḇaq for the enemy’s pursuit — the word that describes righteous clinging to YHWH (Deuteronomy 10:20; 1 Chronicles 11:22) — is perhaps not intentional but sharpens the scene: Saul is overtaken by those who ‘cling’ to him in violence. Jonathan, Abinadab, Malchishua — the three sons who die with their father.
3: wattiḵbad hammiḥlāmāh ʾel šāʾûl — ‘the battle pressed hard against Saul’ (literally ‘was heavy’; the idiom of kāḇēḏ for intensive or burdensome pressure). wayyimṣāʾûhû hammorîm — ‘the archers found/overtook him.’ The root māṣāʾ (to find) used of the archers reaching Saul is suggestive: Saul has been ‘found out,’ located in his moment of abandonment. wayyāḥel mēhammôrîm — ‘and he was wounded by the archers’ (Qal imperfect of ḥûl, to writhe, be in anguish, or in this context to be wounded/pierced).
4: hāʾărālîm hāʾēlleh — ‘these uncircumcised ones.’ The word ʿārēl (uncircumcised) is the ethnic-covenantal boundary marker used throughout Samuel for Philistines as the quintessential covenant outsiders. Saul’s fear is that they will hitʿallᵉlû bô — ‘abuse/mock/make sport of him’ (Hithpael of ʿālal); the same verb is used of Israel’s abuse by Egypt (Exodus 10:2). wayyirrā mallʾōd — ‘and he was very afraid’ (Qal imperfect of yārēʾ). The armor-bearer’s refusal to kill the king (lōʾ ʾāḇāh, ‘he was not willing’) reflects the inviolability of the royal person — even in extremis, striking the anointed is unthinkable (cf. David’s repeated refusals, 1 Samuel 24:6; 1 Chronicles 26:9).
5: The armor-bearer fell on his own sword after Saul — the same word wayyāmot (‘and he died’) for both, the king and his servant. The Chronicler’s terse account omits the detail that Saul fell on his own sword first (1 Samuel 31:4); the Chronicler says simply wayyiqaḥ haḥereḇ wayyippōl ʿālêhā — ‘and he took the sword and fell upon it.’ The armor-bearer’s identical death (wayyāmot) is noted without further comment — he followed his king in death as in service.
6: wayyāmot šāʾûl ûšᵉlōšet bānāw — ‘So Saul died, and his three sons died.’ The Chronicler adds wᵉkol bêtô yaḥdāw — ‘and all his household together’ — a phrase not in 1 Samuel 31:6, which says instead ‘and all his men.’ The Chronicler’s ‘all his household’ underscores the totality of the dynastic collapse; it echoes the language of divine judgment against faithless houses in Kings (1 Kings 21:22; 2 Kings 9:8). The Meribbaal/Mephibosheth narrative is thus largely absent from Chronicles because the Chronicler has written the Saulide household as extinct — the 1 Chronicles 9 genealogy preserving the line is presented as post-narratival continuation.
7: wāyaʿazᵉḇû ʾet ʿārêhem wayyānusû — ‘they abandoned their cities and fled.’ The verb ʿāzaḇ (to abandon, forsake) is significant: the same verb used of Israel’s forsaking of YHWH (Deuteronomy 28:20; 1 Chronicles 28:9) is here applied to Israel’s abandonment of their cities — the two abandonments are related in the Deuteronomistic-Chronicler theology. wayyāḇōʾû ḥaplištîm wayyēšᵉḇû bāhem — ‘the Philistines came and settled in them’; the invader’s occupation of abandoned cities is the concrete embodiment of the covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:30-33).
8: lᵉpōšēṭ ḥᵃlālîm — ‘to strip the slain’ (literally ‘to strip those pierced/fallen’). The stripping of fallen warriors for their weapons and valuables was standard ANE battlefield practice; the same act performed on Saul’s body is a desecration because of his royal status. The Philistines ‘found’ (wayyimṣᵉʾû) Saul and his sons — the same māṣāʾ (to find) as in v3, framing the entire battle sequence: the archers ‘found’ Saul in the battle, the Philistines ‘found’ his body on the field.
9: wayYāsîrû ʾet rōʾšô — ‘they stripped him, took his head.’ Decapitation of a fallen enemy was a trophy act with deep ANE symbolism (David took Goliath’s head, 1 Samuel 17:54). lᵉḇaśśēr ʾet ʿaṣabbêhem — ‘to bring the good news (bāśar) to their idols (ʿaṣabbêhem).’ The word ʿaṣāḇ for idols is pejorative (the same root as ‘pain/toil’ — Psalms 16:4: ‘those who run after another god multiply their sorrows [ʿaṣṣᵉḇōtām]’). The irony is precise: the Philistines bring gospel-news (bāśar, the positive announcement) to objects of grief — the victory news delivered to pain-objects.
10: bêt ʾĕlōhêhem — ‘the house of their gods.’ The Chronicler places the armor in the general ‘temple of their gods’ and Saul’s head ‘in the temple of Dagon’ (bêt dāgôn). 1 Samuel 31:10 reverses the distribution — armor in ‘the house of Ashtaroth,’ body fastened to the wall of Beth-shan. The Chronicler’s focus on Dagon is theologically pointed: Dagon is the god before whom the captured ark humiliated itself (1 Samuel 5:1-5), and now the Philistines hang Saul’s head in Dagon’s house — the same temple that will eventually bear witness to the ark’s power. The theological juxtaposition is part of the Chronicler’s consistent anti-idolatry polemic.
11: wayyišmᵉʿû kol yōšᵉḇê yāḇêš gilʿāḏ — ‘when all the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead heard.’ The men of Jabesh-gilead have a special loyalty to Saul: he rescued them from Nahash the Ammonite at the beginning of his reign (1 Samuel 11:1-11), and they repay that loyalty now. The Chronicler does not explain this bond — it is assumed the reader knows the backstory from Samuel.
12: kol gibbôrê ḥāyil — ‘all the valiant men’ — the same phrase used for capable priestly workers in 1 Chronicles 9:13. Here it describes the Jabesh-gilead warriors who make the night journey to retrieve Saul’s body: wayyiqqᵉḥû ʾet gūpat šāʾûl — ‘they took the corpse (gûpāh) of Saul.’ ûyqibrû ʾet ʿaṣmôtêhem — ‘they buried their bones.’ The shift from ‘bodies’ (1 Samuel 31:12: ʾet gᵉwiyyātō) to ‘bones’ in Chronicles may reflect a later stage (bones after burning/decomposition), or a textual variant. The oak at Jabesh (hāʾēlāh) serves as a funerary marker — trees as burial markers are attested in Genesis 35:8 (Deborah buried under the ‘oak of weeping’).
13: wayyāmot šāʾûl bᵉmaʿᵃlô — ‘Saul died because of his unfaithfulness (maʿal).’ This is the Chronicler’s unique contribution over 1 Samuel — the entire death narrative has been building to this theological verdict. Māʿal (unfaithfulness, covenant breach, treachery) is the Chronicler’s signature evaluative term: it appears 24 times in Chronicles versus only 3 times in the parallel Samuel-Kings material. It denotes specifically the violation of the sacred, a betrayal of covenant with YHWH that carries corporate consequences. Three specific acts of maʿal are identified: (1) ʾăšer māʿal bîdbar YHWH — not keeping YHWH’s command (the Amalekite disobedience, 1 Sam 15); (2) wᵉgam lišʾôl bāʾôḇ lîdrôš — consulting a medium to seek guidance (dāraš); and (3) implicitly from v14, failing to seek YHWH directly. The word māʿal brackets both the Saul death narrative and the exile summary of v1 (bᵉmaʿalām) — exile and royal death share the same root cause.
14: wᵉlōʾ dāraš bîYHWH — ‘and he did not seek YHWH’s guidance.’ The verb dāraš (to seek, inquire, require) is the Chronicler’s evaluative criterion for kings: those who dāraš YHWH are blessed (Asa, 2 Chronicles 14:4; Jehoshaphat, 1 Chronicles 17:3; Hezekiah, 1 Chronicles 31:21; Josiah, 1 Chronicles 34:3); those who do not are judged. Samuel-Kings uses the verb occasionally; the Chronicler systematizes it as the primary criterion of royal faithfulness. The specific charge here — Saul consulted a medium to seek (dāraš) guidance instead of seeking YHWH — places the medium consultation not merely as a ritual violation but as the misuse of the seeking-faculty itself. wayyᵉmîtēhû — ‘therefore he [YHWH] put him to death’ — YHWH is the subject of Saul’s death; the Philistines are the instrument but YHWH is the agent. wayYassēb ʾet hamᵉlûḵāh lᵉdāwid ben yišay — ‘and turned the kingdom to David son of Jesse’ — the kingship transfer is divine action (sāḇaḇ, Hiphil, to turn/redirect), not merely political succession. The chapter ends not with Saul but with David — the dynasty toward which the Chronicler has been orienting the entire genealogical preface.
